content.problemStatement:
Chef loves sorting! Chef recently learnt about a new form of sorting, endsort!

While performing endsort of an array AAA of NNN distinct elements, Chef can move any element to the end of the array.

However, Chef fears that this will take too much time. Help Chef find the number of moves to sort the given array in ascending order using endsort!

###Input:


The first line contains a single integer TTT, the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the number of elements of given array AAA.
The next line would contain NNN space separated integers, the elements of the array.
###Output:

Print a single integer, the minimum number of moves to sort the array in ascending order using endsort.

###Constraints


1≤T≤101 \leq T \leq 101≤T≤10
1≤N≤1000001 \leq N \leq 1000001≤N≤100000
1≤Ai≤1091 \leq A_{i} \leq 10^91≤Ai​≤109
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N distinct integers A1, A2, ..., AN separated by spaces.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of end‑sort moves required to sort the array in ascending order.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First array is already sorted, so 0 moves. In the second array the longest prefix of the sorted order that appears as a subsequence is [1,2]; length 2, so 5‑2 = 3 moves are needed (move 3, then 4, then 5).

solution.approach: Let B be the sorted version of the given array A. While scanning A from left to right we try to match the elements of B starting from its first element. A pointer i (initially 0) denotes how many smallest elements of B have already been found as a subsequence in A. For each a in A, if a == B[i] we increment i. After the scan, i is the length of the longest prefix of the sorted array that appears as a subsequence in the original array. All other elements must be moved to the end; each move places one element at the end, so the minimum number of moves equals N - i. The algorithm consists of sorting (O(N log N)) and a linear scan (O(N)).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case due to sorting; the scan is O(N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the sorted copy of the array.
